# Brief: Amira (PERSONA-013)
## Upcoming Meeting: 2026-03-19

## Since Last Meeting

No new inter-meeting evidence has surfaced in the 10 days since the March 9 meeting.

That silence doesn't mean things have settled. It means everything from March 9 is still exactly where it was: two special education teachers have resigned with no announced replacements, band trips and robotics still require special board approval, Option 1.7 (closing Skillin) is still the leading recommendation with at least four board members publicly opposed, and the June 9 budget adoption deadline is now under 12 weeks away. The 78 positions — including 42 teachers — are still proposed for elimination.

For Amira, the absence of news is the news. Nothing has been taken off the table. Nothing at her school has been explained or promised. The adults are still deciding, and she still doesn't know if the school year she returns to in September will look anything like the one she left in June.

One open question from February has a possible answer tonight: Project Home was approximately 10 days from running out of money as of February 17. An agenda item at tonight's council meeting (I.4) proposes appropriating funds for Project Home. What happened in the interval — and what the council decides tonight — is the closest thing to an answer she will get about whether the immigrant families facing eviction received any real institutional support after the symbolic resolution of February 3 and the 4-2 defeat of Ordinance 17.

---

## Open Questions

These are the unresolved threads Amira carries into tonight's meeting:

- Is any teacher I actually know at Memorial — band director, gifted program teacher, Mrs. Rodriguez — on the list of 42 cuts? Why won't anyone just tell students directly?
- If Skillin closes, what happens to the Somali kids whose families specifically chose that school? Do they just get sent somewhere else and told to be flexible?
- Two special ed teachers quit — are the kids who needed them just going without help now? Who's even tracking that?
- Why did DeAngelis give that whole speech in December about how ignoring students causes real harm — she even told her own middle school story — and then make a massive decision about my school without asking a single student?
- Project Home was almost out of money a month ago. Did those families get evicted? Or did someone actually help them?
- Are families in my neighborhood still too afraid to come to meetings? If they are, who is speaking for them?
- The January goal-setting meeting had no video, no minutes, nothing — so does it even matter if students or families show up to all the other meetings, if the real decisions were already made somewhere no one could see?
- Who is actually responsible for the 34 accounting errors and the $400K that went to the wrong places? Did anyone lose their job, or did the crisis just get handed to teachers and students to deal with?
- The city is spending money on a huge city hall project. Why can the city afford that but not HVAC repairs at Skillin, or paper for classrooms?
- If my school community can't show up because of ICE, and the people who can show up don't look like my family — who is actually making decisions about schools in my neighborhood?

---

## Agenda Implications

### H.1 — City Facilities: Mahoney Decision (Major Item)

**What this means for Amira:** This is the three-way tax competition made concrete and official. The council is deciding whether to move forward with the scaled-down Mahoney project — the same $194M capital project that has been competing with school budget needs and homeowner tax capacity since January. The three questions being put to the council tonight (proceed with scaled-down project? pursue a public/private partnership RFP? address Library facility needs?) are the council deciding how much public money goes toward city buildings at the exact moment school buildings can't afford HVAC repairs.

There is also a detail Amira should hold in mind: the social worker who spoke at the Mahoney workshop warned that placing a police station adjacent to City Hall would deter immigrant families from accessing city services. That warning was delivered in January. Tonight the council is making decisions about that exact building. Whether that warning shaped anything in the design will be visible in what they vote on.

**What to listen for:** Does any councilor name the school budget impact when discussing Mahoney costs? Does anyone acknowledge that proceeding with Mahoney increases tax pressure at the same moment schools are proposing to eliminate 78 positions? Is the police station placement being reconsidered at all, or did the social worker's warning disappear into the record the way the January goal-setting session did?

**Questions to prepare:** *Is anyone going to say out loud that doing Mahoney and cutting 42 teachers is happening at the same time to the same taxpayers?*

---

### E.5 — Budget Workshop Schedule (ORDER #161)

**What this means for Amira:** This order sets the public hearing date and lays out which city departments present their budgets when. The school budget is listed first at Budget Workshop #1 on April 14. That means in less than four weeks, the city council will formally begin deciding what the school budget looks like — and "School" is the first item on their agenda.

This matters because it means the council's first, freshest pass at the budget is the school budget. But it also means that tonight is likely the last regular council meeting before the school budget comes directly before them. If any councilor has something to say about the school budget before April 14 — what they expect, what they want cut, what they're worried about — they might say it tonight, in comments or in discussion of other items.

**What to listen for:** Does any councilor preview their April 14 position tonight? Does anyone repeat the February demand for "up to 50 staff reductions"? Does the school appear in any other agenda item's discussion — even tangentially?

**Questions to prepare:** *Is the April 14 budget workshop going to include any student or family testimony, or is it just department heads talking to councilors?*

---

### I.4 — Project Home Funding Appropriation

**What this means for Amira:** This is the most direct answer she will get to one of her longest-running open questions. Project Home — the fund helping immigrant families facing eviction — was about 10 days from running out of money as of February 17. Tonight the council is being asked to appropriate funds for it. Whether this passes cleanly, gets debated, gets reduced, or fails tells her more than any speech about how much the council values the families it welcomed in December.

This agenda item sits directly alongside everything else she knows: the unanimous but toothless immigrant support resolution from February 3, the 4-2 defeat of Ordinance 17 (the temporary eviction moratorium), the cancelled January forum. All of those were moments when the institution could have acted and didn't — or acted symbolically. A Project Home appropriation that actually passes would be the first time the council has put money, not just words, behind protecting immigrant families in South Portland.

**What to listen for:** How much discussion does this generate? Is it treated as a routine consent item or a contested vote? Does anyone push back on the amount, the conditions, or the principle? Does Councilor Scott — who voted against Ordinance 17 in February — vote yes or no tonight?

**Questions to prepare:** *Is this enough to actually help the families who were facing eviction in February, or is it too late for some of them?*

---

### D (Items 14–22) — Committee Vacancies and Appointments (Postponed to April 7)

**What this means for Amira:** The council is being asked to postpone all 22 appointment calendar items to April 7. This includes five current vacancies (Springer, Maxfield, Duvernay, and two others who resigned in January) and multiple anticipated vacancies. Who fills civic committee positions determines who shapes future decisions — on school policy, on housing, on everything Amira has been tracking. Three of the five current vacancies came from resignations that clustered in a two-week window in January. The council punting all of these to April is another pattern of deferral.

**What to listen for:** Is there any discussion about why all appointments are being postponed, or does this pass without comment? Are any of these committees connected to school oversight, housing, or services that affect Amira's community?

---

### H.1 Library Facilities (within the Mahoney item)

**What this means for Amira:** The council is being asked how it wants to address Library facility needs — this appears as one of three questions embedded in the Mahoney decision. The library matters directly to Amira: she wrote a letter to her principal when library hours were cut and received a form response. If library facility needs get deprioritized or underfunded inside a broader Mahoney decision, that has real consequences for students who use public libraries as an extension of school.

**What to listen for:** Is the library treated as an afterthought in the Mahoney discussion, or does it get specific attention and a funding path?

---

### I.8 — Library Donation Acceptance ($50)

**What this means for Amira:** This is a small procedural item — accepting a $50 donation to the library. On its own, it's unremarkable. But placed next to a facility decision about the library (H.1) and the pattern of underfunding Amira has been tracking, $50 in private donations to keep a public library going is a quiet signal about institutional scarcity. The contrast between what the library gets from the city and what it gets from individual residents is visible if you're looking for it.

---

### E.1, E.2 — Proclamations (Savannah Johnson, Julie Shane)

**What this means for Amira:** These are moments when the council publicly names what it values and who it celebrates. Amira should note who is being recognized and what they did. If either person's work connects to schools, youth, or community services, that's a data point about what the council sees as worth celebrating — even as it weighs deep cuts to those same institutions.

---

### I.1, I.2, I.3 — Postponed Orders from March 3

**What this means for Amira:** These three orders were deferred from the March 3 meeting and are coming back tonight. The content isn't fully specified in the agenda, but the pattern of deferral itself matters. Things keep getting pushed as the June 9 school budget deadline approaches. If any of these involve school-adjacent issues — housing, community services, facilities — that's relevant. The agenda shows amended orders, suggesting these items have already been revised at least once.

**What to listen for:** Are any of these orders related to school facilities, community services, or the property tax competition Amira has been tracking?

---

## Watch For

- **Councilor Scott's votes on I.4 and H.1.** She toured schools in December, named "challenging facilities and resources," and then voted against Ordinance 17 in February. Tonight she votes on Project Home funding and on the Mahoney project that competes with school budget dollars. Her votes are the clearest test of whether her December observations connect to any consistent position — or whether the gap between what she saw and what she votes for keeps widening.

- **Whether any councilor names the school budget while discussing Mahoney.** The connection between the city facilities spending decision and the school budget crisis is logical and documented, but no elected official has yet said both things in the same breath in a public meeting. If someone does tonight, that's new.

- **The level of debate around Project Home (I.4).** If it passes without discussion, that means the council treats immigrant family housing support as uncontroversial — which is progress from February. If it's contested, that tells Amira the symbolic resolution of February 3 was genuinely the ceiling of institutional will, and tonight's appropriation is not a given.

- **Whether the police station placement in the Mahoney design (H.1) gets revisited.** A social worker warned in January that this would deter immigrant families from accessing city services. If tonight's council presentation doesn't address that concern, the warning has been absorbed and ignored — and Amira's family has reason to treat City Hall as less accessible, not more.

- **Any preview of April 14 school budget workshop expectations.** If councilors who demanded "up to 50 staff reductions" in February say anything tonight — in passing, in comments, in questions — that is a signal of what the school board will face in 26 days.

- **Councilor Walker's posture on the Mahoney decision.** Walker was the one who named, near midnight at a Mahoney workshop, the risk of a community that stops investing in schools and libraries. Tonight Walker is being asked to vote on precisely the kind of capital project Walker warned against. Does Walker's caution translate into a vote, or does it disappear the way other warnings have?

- **Whether the library gets its own discussion** within the Mahoney item, or gets absorbed into a single facilities vote. If the council makes a library decision without ever specifically discussing the library's condition, hours, or student use — that's how things Amira cares about disappear from the record without anyone directly voting against them.

- **The room.** Who shows up. Are immigrant community members present, given that the Project Home appropriation is on the agenda? Are teachers or parents there? The presence or absence of the communities most affected by tonight's decisions is itself information about whether civic participation is still possible for the people the budget most affects.
